requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'LAMP: Look-Ahead Mixed-Precision Inference of Large Language Models' have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: Required models, datasets, and infrastructure have been acquired and set up.
The GPT-2 XL pretrained model (1.5B parameters) is loaded from Hugging Face for inference.

verification.spec: Check that the implementation loads 'gpt2-xl' from Hugging Face via transformers.GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained('gpt2-xl') (or AutoModelForCausalLM equivalent) and that the resulting model has the expected ~1.5B parameter count (sum of param.numel() within 1% of 1.5e9).

sub_tasks.requirements: The GPT-2 small pretrained model is loaded from Hugging Face for the model-comparison experiment in Appendix C.2.

verification.spec: Check that the implementation loads 'gpt2' (GPT-2 small, ~124M params) from Hugging Face via transformers.GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained('gpt2') and that the model loads successfully and has ~124M parameters.

sub_tasks.requirements: The OpenWebText dataset (Skylion007/openwebtext) is downloaded and tokenizable for evaluation.

verification.spec: Load the Skylion007/openwebtext dataset via HuggingFace datasets, tokenize a few sequences with the GPT-2 tokenizer, and verify that at least 100 sequences of 1024 tokens can be produced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The CodeParrot dataset (codeparrot/codeparrot-clean) is downloaded and tokenizable for evaluation.

verification.spec: Load the codeparrot/codeparrot-clean dataset via HuggingFace datasets, tokenize a few sequences with the GPT-2 tokenizer, and verify that at least 100 sequences of 1024 tokens can be produced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The ArXiv summarization dataset (ccdv/arxiv-summarization) is downloaded and tokenizable for evaluation.

verification.spec: Load the ccdv/arxiv-summarization dataset via HuggingFace datasets, tokenize a few sequences with the GPT-2 tokenizer, and verify that at least 100 sequences of 1024 tokens can be produced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The WikiText-2 dataset (Salesforce/wikitext) is downloaded and tokenizable for perplexity evaluation.

verification.spec: Load the Salesforce/wikitext (WikiText-2) dataset via HuggingFace datasets, tokenize with the GPT-2 tokenizer, and verify that at least 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens can be produced for perplexity evaluation.

sub_tasks.requirements: The GSM8k Math dataset (openai/gsm8k) is downloaded and tokenizable for perplexity evaluation.

verification.spec: Load the openai/gsm8k dataset via HuggingFace datasets, tokenize with the GPT-2 tokenizer, and verify that at least 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens can be produced for perplexity evaluation.

sub_tasks.requirements: A GPU environment with at least 80GB VRAM (or equivalent) is available to run GPT-2 XL inference experiments.

verification.spec: Verify that torch.cuda.is_available() is True and torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory >= 70 * 1024**3 (at least ~70GB to allow for 80GB VRAM as stated in the paper).

sub_tasks.requirements: PyTorch is installed with CUDA support so that custom matrix-multiplication kernels can be exercised on GPU.

verification.spec: Import torch, assert torch.cuda.is_available() is True, allocate a tensor on cuda, perform a matmul, and verify the result matches CPU computation.

sub_tasks.requirements: The custom partial-single floating-point format PS(µ) and the simulated low-precision matrix product have been implemented per Subsection 4.1.
A function rounding FP32 tensors to PS(µ) with µ ∈ {1,...,23} mantissa bits, 8 exponent bits, 1 sign bit, using round-to-nearest-ties-to-even, has been implemented.

verification.spec: Unit-test the PS(mu) rounding function: for mu in {1,4,7,10,23} and 1000 random FP32 inputs, verify that (a) sign and exponent bits are preserved, (b) mantissa is truncated/rounded to exactly mu bits using round-to-nearest-ties-to-even (compare against a reference implementation using IEEE-754 bit manipulation), (c) at mu=23 the rounding is the identity.

sub_tasks.requirements: PS(23) is bit-equivalent to FP32, PS(10) is equivalent to TF32, and PS(7) is equivalent to BF16 in the rounding function.

verification.spec: Unit test: generate 1000 random FP32 values and verify (a) PS(23) output bitwise equals input, (b) PS(10) output equals casting to TF32 (truncate to 10 mantissa bits with RNE), (c) PS(7) output equals casting to BF16 (rounded). Comparison against torch.bfloat16 cast (after RNE) and a reference TF32 rounder.

sub_tasks.requirements: The general LAMP framework (Algorithm 1) and its objectives have been implemented as described in Section 2.
The componentwise LAMP objective κ_c(f, ŷ; q) = max_i (|J_f(ŷ)|*diag(ŷ)*(I-diag(q))*1)_i / |f(ŷ)|_i has been implemented and is evaluated using computed values of f(ŷ) and J_f(ŷ).

verification.spec: Import the componentwise LAMP objective function (e.g., kappa_c) from the repo. Construct a small test case with a known nonlinearity f (e.g., RMS layer normalization or softmax) and a random vector y_hat of dimension n=8. For several binary masks q (including q=0, q=1, and random sparse masks), compute kappa_c(f, y_hat; q) and verify it equals max_i [ (|J_f(y_hat)| * diag(y_hat) * (I - diag(q))) * 1 ]_i / |f(y_hat)|_i, where |J_f(y_hat)| is computed numerically via autograd or finite differences. Verify (a) kappa_c(q=1)=0, (b) for f = RMS layer normalization the closed-form value from Proposition 3.1 is reproduced within 1e-5, and (c) the implementation accepts computed (not exact) f(y_hat) and J_f(y_hat).

sub_tasks.requirements: The normwise ℓ_p LAMP objective κ_p(f, ŷ; q) bounding the relative normwise rounding error of the composition has been implemented as in Section 2.3.

verification.spec: Import the normwise lp LAMP objective function (e.g., kappa_p) from the repo. Construct a test case with a known nonlinearity f (e.g., softmax) on a random vector y_hat of dimension n=8 with p in {1, 2, inf}. For several binary masks q, compute kappa_p(f, y_hat; q) and verify it bounds the relative normwise lp error ||z_hat - z||_p / ||z||_p in line with Eq. (4) of Section 2.3. Specifically, verify (a) kappa_p(q=1)=0, (b) kappa_p(q=0) equals the mixed condition number of f at y_hat, and (c) for f=softmax with p=1 the closed-form result of Proposition 3.3 (kappa_p = sum_{j not in Omega} z_j (1 - z_j) |y_j| / ||z||_1) is reproduced within 1e-5.

sub_tasks.requirements: The LAMP sparsity-minimization problem (5) ||q||_0 → min s.t. κ(q) ≤ τ is implemented as an interface that downstream nonlinearity-specific solvers (activation, RMSNorm, softmax) can invoke.

verification.spec: Import the LAMP solver module and verify that a generic interface exists (e.g., solve_lamp(kappa_fn, tau) or a base class) that is invoked by the activation, RMS-layernorm, and softmax-specific closed-form solvers in the codebase.

sub_tasks.requirements: Closed-form LAMP solvers for transformer-specific nonlinearities have been implemented per Section 3.

For an entrywise activation function f(y) = [φ(y_1),...,φ(y_n)], the LAMP solver selects entry j iff |φ'(y_j) * y_j / φ(y_j)| > τ, matching the diagonal-Jacobian structure described in Subsection 3.1.

verification.spec: Take an entrywise activation (e.g., GELU) on R^8 with a synthetic y vector. Call the activation LAMP solver with a chosen tau. Verify that the returned q has q_j = 1 iff |phi'(y_j) * y_j / phi(y_j)| > tau, by independently computing this ratio for each j and comparing against the solver's mask.

sub_tasks.requirements: For RMS layer normalization (eq. 7), κ_c is computed using the closed-form expression of Proposition 3.1.

verification.spec: For RMS layer normalization f(y) = y / sqrt(mean(y^2)) and random y in R^8 and various q in {0,1}^8 with q != 1, compute kappa_c using the implementation. Verify the result equals the closed-form expression of Proposition 3.1, which depends on Omega = supp(q) and the squared norm of y restricted to its complement; assert match to 1e-6.

sub_tasks.requirements: The greedy RMSNorm LAMP solver from Proposition 3.2 is implemented: sort entries of y by y_i^2 descending, pick smallest s such that the threshold inequality on the partial sum holds, build q from the corresponding indices.

verification.spec: Given a random y in R^16 and tau > 0, run the greedy RMSNorm solver. Verify: (1) y is sorted so that y_perm[i]^2 is descending; (2) the smallest s such that the threshold inequality holds is chosen; (3) the returned q has 1s at the original indices corresponding to the first s sorted positions. Cross-check by exhaustively trying all q with at most s+1 nonzeros and confirming none with strictly fewer than s nonzeros satisfies kappa_c(q) <= tau.

sub_tasks.requirements: For softmax (eq. 6), the ℓ_1 normwise LAMP solution of Proposition 3.3 / eq. (8) is implemented: the j-th entry of q is set to 1 iff z_j * |y_j| * (1 - z_j) > τ / 2 (or the equivalent inequality given in the paper).

verification.spec: Construct a unit test: given a random pre-softmax vector y, compute z=softmax(y), then call the strict LAMP selector. Verify it returns a mask q where q_j == 1 iff z_j * |y_j| * (1 - z_j) > tau/2 (eq. 8 condition). Test on multiple y vectors including concentrated and uniform distributions.

sub_tasks.requirements: The relaxed relative-threshold LAMP for softmax (eq. 9, Subsection 4.4) is implemented by dropping the (1 - z_j) factor and using a relative threshold 0 ≤ τ < 1 that is independent of the softmax normalization constant.

verification.spec: Unit test the relaxed LAMP selector: given y and z=softmax(y), verify q_j == 1 iff z_j * |y_j| > tau (eq. 9, with (1 - z_j) factor dropped, tau a relative threshold in [0,1)). Verify that scaling y by adding a constant c to every entry (which leaves softmax unchanged) does not change the selection, demonstrating independence from the normalization constant.

sub_tasks.requirements: A length-normalized variant of the relaxed relative-threshold LAMP is implemented in which the per-row threshold is scaled by τ*sqrt(1024/n), where n is the row length (Appendix C.5).

verification.spec: Unit test: for each causal row of length n, the length-normalized relaxed LAMP uses effective threshold tau * sqrt(1024 / n) instead of tau. Verify that for n=1024 the LN threshold equals tau, and for n=256 it equals 2*tau, and that selected indices satisfy z_j * |y_j| > tau*sqrt(1024/n).

sub_tasks.requirements: LAMP has been integrated into GPT-2 attention so that KQ inner products are computed in PS(µ) and selectively recomputed in FP32.
After PS(µ) accumulation, the softmax probabilities z and KQ values y are materialized so that the LAMP selection rule (8) or (9) can be applied per row of the causal KQ matrix.

verification.spec: Run a single GPT-2 XL attention forward pass with PS(µ) KQ accumulation and assert that the softmax probability tensor z and the KQ score tensor y are accessible (materialized) at the point where the LAMP selection rule is applied, for at least one head/row.

sub_tasks.requirements: The causal mask is respected when counting recomputable inner products: only entries above/at the diagonal of the causal KQ matrix are considered candidates for selection.

verification.spec: Run LAMP attention on a synthetic causal input. Inspect the set of indices flagged for recomputation and assert that none of them lie strictly above the diagonal (i.e., all candidates respect the causal mask), and that the denominator used for the recomputation rate counts only causal entries n(n+1)/2.

sub_tasks.requirements: A random-recomputation baseline is implemented in which the per-row number of recomputed inner products is taken from the LAMP solver but the specific indices are sampled uniformly at random.

verification.spec: Unit test: for a batch of attention rows, run the LAMP solver to get k_i recomputed indices per row i. Then the random baseline must produce a mask with exactly k_i ones per row drawn uniformly at random (without replacement) over valid (non-masked) positions. Verify on multiple seeds that index distributions are uniform and counts match LAMP exactly.

sub_tasks.requirements: A no-recomputation baseline is implemented in which KQ inner products are accumulated only in PS(µ).

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on a small OpenWebText sample (e.g. 4 sequences of 1024 tokens) with the no-recomputation baseline configured to mu=7. Verify (i) outputs differ from FP32 reference (KL > 0), (ii) reported sparsity is 0%, (iii) outputs match a reference implementation that uniformly rounds the KQ accumulator to PS(mu=7).

sub_tasks.requirements: A full-precision reference attention is implemented in which all KQ inner products accumulate in FP32, used as the ground-truth model.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL FP32 reference on 4 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens, verifying (i) the output logits agree bitwise with the HuggingFace FP32 forward pass to within 1e-5 max absolute error, (ii) KL divergence of this model against itself is 0, (iii) this model is used as the reference distribution in the KL-divergence metric.

sub_tasks.requirements: Evaluation metrics and harness for LAMP inference are implemented per Subsection 4.2 and Appendix C.
Mean Kullback–Leibler divergence between reference (FP32) and test next-token distributions, averaged over 100 sequences of 1024 tokens each, is implemented.

verification.spec: Construct two synthetic next-token distribution tensors of shape (100, 1024, V). Call the implemented KL-divergence routine. Verify that it returns the mean KL averaged over 100 sequences × 1024 token positions, and that the result matches a reference numpy/torch implementation to 1e-6.

sub_tasks.requirements: Flip rate (fraction of token positions at which the argmax of the reference and test distributions differ) is implemented over the same 100 sequences of 1024 tokens.

verification.spec: Construct two synthetic next-token distribution tensors of shape (100, 1024, V). Call the flip-rate routine and verify it returns the fraction of (sequence, position) pairs where argmax of reference differs from argmax of test, matching a numpy reference implementation exactly.

sub_tasks.requirements: The recomputation rate (sparsity) is computed as the number of KQ inner products selected by LAMP divided by the total number of unmasked KQ inner products in the causal mask.

verification.spec: Run LAMP attention on a synthetic input with known causal mask of size n. Count the number of selected indices reported by LAMP and divide by n(n+1)/2 (the number of causal-mask entries). Verify that this matches the recomputation rate reported by the framework.

sub_tasks.requirements: Perplexity is computed over 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens for the perplexity experiments in Appendix C.5.

verification.spec: Invoke the perplexity-evaluation routine and verify by inspecting its inputs/outputs (or via logs) that it processes exactly 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens each from the chosen dataset, and that the returned perplexity equals exp(mean NLL) over those 100×1024 tokens to within numerical precision.

sub_tasks.requirements: An experiment driver sweeps mantissa bits µ ∈ {4, 7, 10, 13, 16, 19, 22} and thresholds τ ∈ {50, 0.5, 0.05, 0.01} (and other values used in Figures 1–3) and logs KL divergence, flip rate, and recomputation rate.

verification.spec: Inspect the experiment driver code/configs and verify that it sweeps mantissa bits µ over {4, 7, 10, 13, 16, 19, 22} and thresholds τ over {50, 0.5, 0.05, 0.01} (plus the threshold sweep used in Figs. 1-3), and that for each (µ,τ) it logs KL divergence, flip rate, and recomputation rate.

sub_tasks.requirements: The headline Figure 1 result for GPT-2 XL on OpenWebText has been reproduced.

With τ = 0.1 and µ = 7, adaptive LAMP achieves KL divergence comparable to uniform TF32 (µ = 10) accumulation against the FP32 reference, while no-recomputation BF16 is orders of magnitude worse (Figure 1 trends).

verification.spec: Measure KL divergence to FP32 reference of (a) adaptive LAMP with mu=7 and tau=0.1 (~0.9% recomputation) and (b) uniform mu=10 (TF32) accumulation, both on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens with GPT-2 XL. Report ratio (a)/(b); paper indicates these should be within ~1 order of magnitude of each other, while no-recomputation mu=7 KL is ~100x larger than (b).

sub_tasks.requirements: With τ = 0.1 and µ = 7, random recomputation at the same 0.9% rate provides essentially no improvement over no recomputation (Figure 1).

verification.spec: Measure KL divergence to FP32 reference for GPT-2 XL on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens with (a) no recomputation at mu=7 and (b) random recomputation at the same ~0.9% rate at mu=7. Report ratio KL(b)/KL(a); paper claims this ratio should be approximately 1 (random recomputation gives no measurable improvement).

sub_tasks.requirements: The effective mantissa bits per KQ inner product under adaptive BF16+0.9% FP32 is approximately 8.9 and under uniform TF32 is 10 (Figure 1 caption arithmetic).

verification.spec: Compute effective mantissa bits = (1 - r) * mu + r * 23 for adaptive BF16 (mu=7) with r=0.9% recomputation rate. Assert this equals ~8.909 bits. Also verify uniform TF32 case = 10.0 bits.

sub_tasks.requirements: The threshold/mantissa sweep results in Figure 2 have been reproduced.
Sweeping µ ∈ {4,7,10,13,16,19,22} for τ ∈ {50, 0.5, 0.05, 0.01} on OpenWebText, KL divergence, flip rate, and sparsity are logged for adaptive LAMP.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL inference on 100 sequences of 1024 tokens from OpenWebText for all combinations of mu in {4,7,10,13,16,19,22} and tau in {50, 0.5, 0.05, 0.01} using adaptive LAMP for KQ accumulation. Verify that for each (mu, tau) pair the script outputs KL divergence (vs FP32 reference), flip rate, and sparsity (recomputation rate) and that results match Figure 2 top row qualitatively (monotone decrease with smaller tau).

sub_tasks.requirements: Sweeping τ over a logarithmic range (10^-3 to 10^3) for µ ∈ {4, 7, 10} on OpenWebText, KL divergence, flip rate, and sparsity are logged for adaptive LAMP.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens, sweeping tau on a log grid covering 10^-3 to 10^3 (at least 7 points per decade) for mu in {4,7,10}. Verify the script logs KL divergence, flip rate, and sparsity per (mu, tau) and reproduces the curves in Figure 2 bottom row (monotone trends, KL saturates for small tau).

sub_tasks.requirements: At small µ the LAMP method achieves approximately 12× reduction in KL divergence at a recomputation rate of approximately 0.3%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens with mu in {4, 5, 6, 7} (small mu). For each mu, compute mean KL divergence with and without LAMP (at the tau yielding ~0.3% recomputation rate). Compute the ratio KL(no recomp) / KL(LAMP). Verify ratio is approximately 12x at recomputation rate ~0.3%.

sub_tasks.requirements: At small µ the LAMP method achieves approximately 83× reduction in KL divergence at a recomputation rate of approximately 1.6%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens with small mu (e.g., 4). Compare mean KL divergence with and without LAMP at the tau yielding ~1.6% recomputation rate. Verify ratio KL(no recomp) / KL(LAMP) ~= 83.

sub_tasks.requirements: At small µ the LAMP method achieves approximately 385× reduction in KL divergence at a recomputation rate of approximately 7.6%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on 100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens with small mu (e.g., 4). Compare mean KL divergence with and without LAMP at the tau yielding ~7.6% recomputation rate. Verify ratio KL(no recomp) / KL(LAMP) ~= 385.

sub_tasks.requirements: The recorded sparsity (recomputation rate) for adaptive LAMP varies only weakly with µ at a fixed τ, consistent with the Jacobian-stability claim.

verification.spec: From the GPT-2 XL OpenWebText sweep, at a fixed tau (e.g. tau=0.05), collect recorded sparsity (recomputation rate) for mu in {4,7,10,13,16}. Compute ratio max(sparsity)/min(sparsity) across mu values; paper claim is this ratio is small (~within factor 2).

sub_tasks.requirements: Both KL divergence and flip rate decay approximately exponentially with decreasing τ (Figure 2 second row).

verification.spec: Using GPT-2 XL adaptive LAMP results on OpenWebText with mu in {4,7,10}, fit log(KL) and log(flip_rate) vs log(tau) over the swept range. Assert the resulting curves are monotonically decreasing in tau and approximately linear in log-log over at least 2 decades (R^2 > 0.85), consistent with exponential decay shown in Figure 2 bottom row.

sub_tasks.requirements: For sufficiently large µ, the KL divergence saturates at the FP32 accuracy limit.

verification.spec: From the mu sweep on OpenWebText with small tau (e.g. tau=0.01), assert that KL divergence stops decreasing for mu >= ~16 and saturates at a value at most ~10x larger than the KL between two FP32 runs differing only by computation order. Equivalently, KL(mu=22) and KL(mu=19) are within a small factor (<5x) of each other.

sub_tasks.requirements: Appendix C.1 dataset-comparison results have been reproduced.
Pareto boundaries (KL divergence vs sparsity, flip rate vs sparsity) are produced for GPT-2 XL with µ = 4 on OpenWebText, CodeParrot, and ArXiv Science.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with mu=4 LAMP attention on 100 sequences of 1024 tokens from each of OpenWebText, CodeParrot, and ArXiv-summarization, sweeping tau. Verify three Pareto curves (one per dataset) are produced for KL divergence vs sparsity and flip rate vs sparsity (Figure 4).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Pareto boundaries on OpenWebText, CodeParrot, and ArXiv almost coincide, supporting the 'input-agnostic' claim.

verification.spec: Compute Pareto boundaries (KL vs sparsity) for GPT-2 XL with mu=4 LAMP on OpenWebText, CodeParrot, and ArXiv. At matched sparsity, compute max pairwise log10 ratio of KL between datasets. Assert this gap is <= 0.5 (curves nearly coincide).

sub_tasks.requirements: Appendix C.2 model-comparison results have been reproduced.
Pareto boundaries are produced for GPT-2 XL and GPT-2 small with µ = 4 on OpenWebText.

verification.spec: Run both GPT-2 XL and GPT-2 small with mu=4 LAMP attention on OpenWebText (100 sequences of 1024 tokens), sweeping tau. Verify two Pareto boundaries (one per model) are produced for KL divergence vs sparsity and flip rate vs sparsity (Figure 5).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Pareto boundary for GPT-2 XL lies below that for GPT-2 small (LAMP performs better on the larger model).

verification.spec: Compute Pareto (KL vs sparsity) for GPT-2 XL and GPT-2 small with mu=4 LAMP on OpenWebText. At a representative matched sparsity (e.g., 0.1% and 1%), compute sign(log10(KL_small / KL_XL)). Assert this is positive at the majority of matched sparsity levels (XL boundary below small's).

sub_tasks.requirements: Appendix C.3 permuted-token results have been reproduced.
A pipeline is implemented that randomly permutes the tokens within each evaluation sequence while preserving the unigram distribution.

verification.spec: Implement a token-permutation function. Apply to OpenWebText sequences of length 1024; verify (a) output sequences contain the same multiset of tokens (unigram distribution preserved per sequence), (b) ordering differs from the input with high probability, (c) permutation is uniform random (sanity check on multiple seeds).

sub_tasks.requirements: Pareto boundaries are produced for GPT-2 XL with µ = 4 on OpenWebText with both direct and permuted token order; the KL boundaries essentially overlap and the flip-rate boundary shifts slightly upward for permuted tokens.

verification.spec: Compute Pareto boundaries for GPT-2 XL, mu=4 LAMP on OpenWebText for (a) original token order and (b) within-sequence random permutation of tokens. At matched sparsity, log10(KL_perm / KL_direct) should be near 0 (within 0.3), while log10(FlipRate_perm / FlipRate_direct) should be slightly positive on average.

sub_tasks.requirements: Appendix C.4 adaptive-vs-random recomputation comparison has been reproduced.
Pareto boundaries comparing adaptive LAMP (8) to a random-recomputation variant (same count, randomly chosen indices) are produced for GPT-2 XL with µ = 4 on OpenWebText.

verification.spec: Run two LAMP configurations on GPT-2 XL µ=4 on OpenWebText: (1) adaptive LAMP (Eq. 8) selecting the top-scoring KQ indices, (2) a variant that selects the SAME NUMBER of indices uniformly at random. Sweep τ and produce Pareto plots of KL vs sparsity and flip rate vs sparsity containing both curves.

sub_tasks.requirements: Random recomputation provides essentially no improvement over no recomputation, confirming that the adaptive choice (not the count) is the source of accuracy gain.

verification.spec: For GPT-2 XL with mu=4 on OpenWebText, compare Pareto boundaries of adaptive LAMP versus random recomputation at counts dictated by LAMP. Assert ratio KL_random_at_rate_r / KL_norecomp_mu4 is ~ 1.0 (random ineffective), while KL_adaptive / KL_norecomp is << 1 at the same rate.

sub_tasks.requirements: Appendix C.5 perplexity table has been reproduced (Table 1).
Full-precision GPT-2 XL perplexity on GSM8k Math is approximately 13.088.

verification.spec: Load GPT-2 XL (openai-community/gpt2-xl) in FP32 and evaluate perplexity on GSM8k Math by computing the average cross-entropy loss over 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens each, then exponentiating. All FP operations should be in full precision (FP32). Verify the resulting perplexity is approximately 13.088 within tolerance 0.02.

sub_tasks.requirements: Low-precision (µ=4, no recomputation) GPT-2 XL perplexity on GSM8k Math is approximately 13.161.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL inference on the GSM8k Math dataset using PS(µ=4) low-precision KQ accumulation with no LAMP recomputation. Compute perplexity over 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens each. Assert mean perplexity is approximately 13.161.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LAMP at τ = 0.03 on GSM8k Math achieves perplexity ≈ 13.091 at sparsity ≈ 5.8%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL inference on GSM8k Math with PS(µ=4) KQ accumulation and the relaxed relative-threshold LAMP rule (eq. 9) at τ = 0.03. Compute perplexity over 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens each. Assert perplexity ≈ 13.091 and that recomputation sparsity is ≈ 5.8%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LN LAMP at τ = 0.03 on GSM8k Math achieves perplexity ≈ 13.094 at sparsity ≈ 4.5%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with µ=4 KQ accumulation and relaxed length-normalized LAMP (Eq. 9 with τ scaled by √(1024/n)) at τ=0.03 on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from GSM8k Math. Compute mean perplexity and recomputation rate; verify perplexity ≈ 13.094 and sparsity ≈ 4.5%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LAMP at τ = 0.09 on GSM8k Math achieves perplexity ≈ 13.093 at sparsity ≈ 2.8%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with µ=4 KQ accumulation and relaxed LAMP (Eq. 9) at τ=0.09 on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from GSM8k Math; compute mean perplexity and recomputation rate. Verify perplexity ≈ 13.093 and sparsity ≈ 2.8%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LN LAMP at τ = 0.09 on GSM8k Math achieves perplexity ≈ 13.096 at sparsity ≈ 2.0%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with µ=4 KQ accumulation and relaxed LN LAMP (Eq. 9 with τ scaled by √(1024/n)) at τ=0.09 on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from GSM8k Math; compute mean perplexity and recomputation rate. Verify perplexity ≈ 13.096 and sparsity ≈ 2.0%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Full-precision GPT-2 XL perplexity on WikiText-2 is approximately 17.411.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL inference on WikiText-2 (Salesforce/wikitext) with uniform FP32 arithmetic. Compute perplexity over 100 consecutive batches of 1024 tokens each. Assert mean perplexity is approximately 17.411.

sub_tasks.requirements: Low-precision (µ=4, no recomputation) GPT-2 XL perplexity on WikiText-2 is approximately 17.524.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with PS(4) KQ accumulation (no recomputation) on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from WikiText-2; compute mean perplexity. Verify ≈ 17.524.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LAMP at τ = 0.03 on WikiText-2 achieves perplexity ≈ 17.454 at sparsity ≈ 5.7%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with µ=4 KQ accumulation and relaxed LAMP (Eq. 9) at τ=0.03 on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from WikiText-2; compute mean perplexity and recomputation rate. Verify perplexity ≈ 17.454 and sparsity ≈ 5.7%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Relaxed LN LAMP at τ = 0.03 on WikiText-2 achieves perplexity ≈ 17.456 at sparsity ≈ 4.3%.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL on OpenWebText with mu=4 and (i) no recomputation, (ii) random recomputation at rate matching LAMP, (iii) LAMP adaptive recomputation; assert KL_random / KL_no_recompute is in [0.5, 2] (no improvement) and KL_LAMP / KL_random <= 0.1
verification.paper_cite: Figure 1 / Figure 7 / Appendix C.4: 'random recomputations do not lead to any improvements'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the length-normalized relaxed LAMP variant (tau=0.03 scaled by sqrt(1024/n)) on GPT-2 XL + GSM8k Math at mu=4 yields perplexity 13.094 at sparsity ~4.5%

verification.spec: Implement LN-thresholded relaxed LAMP that scales tau by sqrt(1024/n) per row; run GPT-2 XL inference on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from GSM8k Math with KQ accumulation in PS(4); compute perplexity and sparsity; assert |perplexity - 13.094|/13.094 <= 0.05 and sparsity ~4.5%
verification.paper_cite: Appendix C.5, Table 1, GSM8k Math, Relaxed LN tau=0.03

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that random recomputation at the same rate as adaptive LAMP yields essentially no KL divergence improvement (random Pareto boundary remains close to no-recomputation baseline) on GPT-2 XL + OpenWebText with mu=4

verification.spec: For GPT-2 XL with KQ accumulation in PS(4) on 100 OpenWebText sequences (length 1024), compare LAMP-adaptive recomputation vs random recomputation at the same rate across a sweep of thresholds; assert at recomputation rate ~1% the adaptive KL divergence is at least 10x lower than random's KL divergence

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x KL divergence ratio (random/adaptive at ~1% rate)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the relaxed relative-threshold LAMP (eq. 9) Pareto boundary lies only marginally above the strict LAMP (eq. 8) Pareto boundary on GPT-2 XL + OpenWebText with mu=4

verification.spec: Sweep thresholds for both strict LAMP (eq 8) and relaxed LAMP (eq 9) on GPT-2 XL + OpenWebText (100 seqs of 1024 tokens) with KQ in PS(4); construct Pareto boundary of KL divergence vs recomputation rate; assert at any matched recomputation rate, the relaxed-LAMP KL divergence is at most 3x the strict-LAMP KL divergence

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x maximum KL ratio relaxed/strict at matched sparsity

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that GPT-2 small with LAMP applied (mu=4) achieves a Pareto boundary on OpenWebText that is higher than that of GPT-2 XL, validating the larger-model-benefits claim

verification.spec: Run LAMP evaluation (eq 8) on both GPT-2 XL and GPT-2 small with KQ accumulation in PS(4) on 100 OpenWebText sequences of length 1024; sweep thresholds; assert at matched recomputation rate (~1%), GPT-2 small's KL divergence is greater than GPT-2 XL's KL divergence

sub_tasks.paper_unit: ratio (GPT-2 small KL / GPT-2 XL KL) > 1

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the recomputation rate of relaxed LAMP (eq. 9) is computed without depending on the softmax normalization constant (the per-row sums of exp(y))

verification.spec: Construct a synthetic KQ row y of length n; compute the relaxed LAMP index set using eq. (9) implementation; multiply y by an arbitrary additive constant c (equivalent to scaling exp normalization); verify the chosen index set is identical, demonstrating independence from the partition function Σ exp(y_i)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the Pareto boundary of length-normalized relaxed LAMP (scaling τ by sqrt(1024/n)) is compared against the uniform-threshold relaxed LAMP on at least one dataset, with both producing similar perplexity reductions over PS(μ=4) low-precision inference

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL with PS(4) KQ accumulation and (a) relaxed LAMP τ=0.03 and (b) relaxed LN-LAMP τ=0.03 (per-row threshold τ·sqrt(1024/n)) on 100 batches of 1024 tokens from CodeParrot; compute perplexity for both; assert each is within 0.02 of 3.180 (paper value); assert LN variant uses fewer recomputations (4.2% vs 5.5%)

sub_tasks.requirements: Method components (from critique splits and additions)

Code has been implemented and run such that a custom partial single PS(mu) floating-point format with mu mantissa bits, 8 exponent bits, and 1 sign bit is implemented via FP32 rounded to mu mantissa bits with round-to-nearest-ties-to-even, and PS(23)==FP32, PS(10)==TF32, PS(7)==BF16

verification.spec: Import the PS(mu) rounding function; verify on random FP32 inputs that PS(23) is an identity, PS(10) matches TF32 rounding, PS(7) matches BF16 rounding (round-to-nearest-ties-to-even) within representable precision; verify that for arbitrary mu in {1..23}, mantissa bits beyond position mu are zeroed
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.1: 'we simulate low-precision matrix products using a custom partial single FP format... PS(mu) numbers via FP32 numbers rounded to mu mantissa bits according to the round-to-nearest-ties-to-even mode'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that mixed-precision matrix multiplication with input-output formats PS(muA) x PS(muB) -> PS(muC) accumulates inner products as round(c + a*b) with scalar multiplication and addition in FP32

verification.spec: Call the mixed-precision matmul function with small inputs in PS(muA), PS(muB); compare against a reference implementation that does element-wise round(c + a*b) in FP32 then rounds the final result to PS(muC); assert exact equality
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.1: 'we accumulate inner products as round(c + a · b), where the scalar multiplication and addition are in FP32'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the 'strict' LAMP solution for softmax selects index j iff (1-z_j) * z_j > tau / max_i((1-z_i)*z_i_complement_term) per Eq. (8), recomputing those KQ inner products in FP32

verification.spec: Construct synthetic softmax probability vectors (concentrated, uniform, and bimodal); call the LAMP selection routine with threshold tau; verify that selected indices match the closed-form criterion of Eq. (8) derived from Proposition 3.3; verify zero selections for a near-one-hot distribution

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the relaxed relative-threshold LAMP for softmax (Eq. 9) selects KQ indices using a criterion independent of the normalization constant sum_i exp(y_i)

verification.spec: Generate KQ logit vectors; apply the relaxed LAMP selection at threshold tau; verify that scaling all logits by adding the same constant (which leaves softmax unchanged) does not change selected indices; verify selection matches the relative-threshold criterion of Eq. (9)
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.4 / Eq. (9): 'complete independence from the normalization constant'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to verify the relative normwise ℓp error bound derivation for compositions, specifically reproducing equation (4) which bounds the normwise rounding error for the composition f(g(x))

verification.spec: Import the LAMP module; verify a function exists that computes κp(f, y; q) as the relative normwise ℓp condition number defined in equation (4); check signature accepts (f, y, q, p) and returns a scalar.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the softmax function (6) as defined in the paper for use within the LAMP framework

verification.spec: Import the softmax implementation; call on a random input vector y of length 10; verify outputs sum to 1, are all positive, and match torch.softmax(y, dim=-1) within 1e-6.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the Jacobian M of an entrywise activation function showing it is diagonal with entries φ'(yi)yi/φ(yi)

verification.spec: Call the activation-LAMP Jacobian function on a vector y of length 8 with a chosen φ (e.g., GELU); verify the returned matrix is diagonal and matches numerically-computed φ'(yi)*yi/φ(yi) within 1e-5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for layer normalization (without scale/bias) standardizing input by subtracting mean and dividing by std

verification.spec: Call layer_norm on a random vector y of length 16; verify output has zero mean and unit variance within 1e-5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for RMS layer normalization f(y) = y / sqrt(mean(y^2))

verification.spec: Call rms_norm on random vector y of length 16; verify output equals y / sqrt(sum(y^2)/n) within 1e-6.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute κc(f, y; q) for RMS layer normalization according to the closed-form expression in Proposition 3.1

verification.spec: Construct a random vector y of length 10 and binary mask q with q != 1; compute κc from the closed-form expression in Proposition 3.1; compare against direct numerical computation of max row-sum of |M(I - diag(q))|; assert agreement within 1e-5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the greedy closed-form solution q' of the componentwise LAMP problem for RMS layer normalization as described in Proposition 3.2

verification.spec: On a vector y of length 20 sorted with y1^2 >= ... >= yn^2, run the greedy algorithm with threshold τ=0.1; verify the constructed q' satisfies κc(f, y; q') <= τ; also verify that q' is obtained by selecting smallest s such that the cumulative condition holds.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to verify the closed-form greedy threshold condition for RMS LAMP selection (entries are recomputed when their squared value passes a derived threshold relative to ‖y‖²)

verification.spec: Implement the threshold rule in Proposition 3.2 (pick smallest s such that the partial sum condition holds), call on a 'spread-out' vector (y1=...=y_{n-1}=1, yn=0) with n=10, τ=0.5; verify s = ceil((2-τ)(n-1)) = ceil(1.5*9) = 14, clamped to feasibility; also test the outlier case (y1=1, others=0) yields s=1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the attention composition f(y) = V * softmax(K^T Q) supporting LAMP evaluation of softmax

verification.spec: Construct random V, K, Q matrices and verify the LAMP attention forward pass matches reference torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention (without scaling) within 1e-4.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute κ1(f, y; q) for softmax according to the closed-form expression in Proposition 3.3 involving products zj(1-zj)|yj|

verification.spec: On a random y of length 16 and random binary q, compute κ1 from the closed-form in Proposition 3.3; compare against the direct ℓ1-normwise condition number expression; assert agreement within 1e-5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the 'strict' LAMP selection rule (8) for softmax: the j-th entry of q is nonzero iff zj(1-zj)|yj| exceeds the τ-threshold

verification.spec: Construct a vector y where one entry dominates (z close to standard basis); verify the strict LAMP selection (8) returns q=0 (no recomputation needed). Construct a uniform y (all KQ products equal); verify many indices are selected.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the custom partial single floating-point format PS(mu) is implemented correctly: for mu in {1..23}, numbers are FP32 values rounded to mu mantissa bits using round-to-nearest-ties-to-even, and PS(23)=FP32, PS(10)=TF32, PS(7)=BF16

verification.spec: Import the PS(mu) rounding function; for mu=23, assert round-trip of random FP32 tensor is bit-identical; for mu=7, assert outputs match BF16 cast for representable values; for mu=10, assert outputs match TF32 rounding; for general mu, verify that the function zeros out mantissa bits below position mu using round-to-nearest-ties-to-even on a small battery of edge cases (powers of two, halfway cases, denormals avoided since exponent width is 8)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the per-row LAMP selection function produces the index set predicted by eq. (8) (strict softmax LAMP) on a synthetic KQ row

verification.spec: Construct a synthetic KQ-row y of length 16; compute softmax z = softmax(y); for a threshold τ=0.1, compute the optimal index set defined by 'j selected iff z_j*(1-z_j) > τ * max_i z_i*(1-z_i)' (eq. 8 form, derived from Prop. 3.3); compare with the implementation's chosen mask; assert exact set equality. Repeat for a concentrated distribution (one dominant entry) and assert the returned set is empty

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that for KQ entries selected by LAMP, the PS(μ) low-precision values are replaced by FP32-recomputed inner products while all non-selected entries remain at their PS(μ) values

verification.spec: On a single attention head with K, Q tensors of shape (d, n), compute (a) PS(4) accumulated KQ matrix M_low; (b) FP32 accumulated KQ matrix M_high; (c) LAMP mask Q (boolean); (d) the LAMP-evaluated KQ matrix M_lamp; assert M_lamp[Q] == M_high[Q] (within FP32 eps) and M_lamp[~Q] == M_low[~Q] exactly

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements LAMP Algorithm 1 end-to-end such that, after recomputing the selected components of the inner function output ŷ in higher precision, the outer function (e.g., softmax) is recomputed using the substituted ŷ and the downstream matmul with V proceeds in the configured low precision; the substitution is reflected in the final attention output.

verification.spec: Construct a small attention block with known K, Q, V and a chosen τ. Run LAMP attention; assert that (a) softmax probabilities for the indices flagged by q are recomputed using FP32 KQ values (match FP32-reference values at those positions to ~FP32 precision), (b) softmax probabilities at non-flagged indices match the low-precision PS(μ) computation, and (c) the final V·softmax product is computed using the configured low-precision matmul (matches a manual low-precision recomputation).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code computes the baseline ŷ = g(x) in the configured low precision (PS(μ)) and returns it as the starting point of LAMP before any recomputation step.

verification.spec: Call the baseline-evaluation function with a known matrix A and vector x at μ=7 (BF16-equivalent). Assert the returned ŷ is bit-identical to a manual PS(7) matmul (FP32 scalar mul-add then round-to-7-mantissa-bits per accumulation step), and differs from the FP32 reference by at most the expected PS(7) rounding error.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code sets up the function κ(q) from f and ŷ supporting both the componentwise objective κ_c and the ℓp-normwise objective κ_p, selectable via an argument.

verification.spec: For f=softmax with a small ŷ and a chosen q, call the κ-setup function in both modes ('componentwise' and 'l1-normwise'). Assert returned values match the closed-form expressions from Proposition 3.3 (for ℓ1) and the formula in Proposition 3.1 / Appendix B (for componentwise) within 1e-5 relative error.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code finds a sparse binary vector q satisfying κ(q) ≤ τ using the closed-form solutions derived for transformer nonlinearities (softmax: equation (8); RMS layernorm: Proposition 3.2 greedy sort).

verification.spec: For softmax with a hand-crafted ŷ where the analytic solution (8) is known, call the solver with τ=0.1 and assert q matches the closed-form mask exactly. Verify κ(q) ≤ τ and that q has fewer set bits than q' obtained by removing any one of its set bits (i.e., q is minimal under the constraint).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code recomputes the components of ŷ indexed by the nonzeros of q in higher precision (FP32) and substitutes them into ŷ before evaluating f.

verification.spec: Given baseline ŷ in PS(μ=4) and a hand-picked q, call the recomputation function. Assert (a) entries at q==1 positions exactly equal the FP32 reference KQ inner products, (b) entries at q==0 positions equal the original PS(4) baseline (unchanged), and (c) a counter or returned recomputation count equals sum(q).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code performs each accumulation step as round(c + a·b) where the scalar multiplication a·b and addition c + (a·b) are carried out in FP32, regardless of the input formats PS(μ_A), PS(μ_B).

verification.spec: Mock or hook the inner-product accumulation function. Pass two PS(4) inputs whose product would underflow PS(4) but is representable in FP32. Assert the intermediate a·b and c+a·b values observed (before the final round to PS(μ_C)) are bit-identical to the corresponding FP32 computation.

sub_tasks.requirements: After each accumulation step, the partial sum is rounded to PS(μ_C) using round-to-nearest-ties-to-even on the FP32 representation.

verification.spec: Construct a sequence of accumulations whose exact FP32 partial sums have known mantissas just past the μ_C-th bit (including halfway cases). Run the kernel with μ_C in {4,7,10,23}. Assert each partial-sum has all mantissa bits beyond position μ_C equal to zero (i.e., is representable in PS(μ_C)), and halfway cases round to the even μ_C-th-bit value.

sub_tasks.requirements: The custom matmul kernel accepts arbitrary (μ_A, μ_B, μ_C) with μ ∈ {1,...,23}, and verifies that inputs in PS(μ_A), PS(μ_B) and outputs in PS(μ_C) are correctly represented (mantissa bits beyond μ are zero; equivalent to FP32 at μ=23, TF32 at μ=10, BF16 at μ=7).

verification.spec: For (μ_A, μ_B, μ_C) ∈ {(7,7,7), (10,10,10), (23,23,23), (4,7,10)}, generate random FP32 inputs, cast to PS(μ_A)/PS(μ_B) via the format helper, run the matmul. Assert: (1) cast inputs have mantissa bits beyond μ equal to zero; (2) output has mantissa bits beyond μ_C equal to zero; (3) at μ_A=μ_B=μ_C=23, result matches torch.matmul in FP32 to within 1e-6 relative error; (4) at μ=7, result matches a reference BF16 emulation (round-to-nearest-even at 7 mantissa bits) bitwise.

Code has been implemented and run such that the relative rounding error of the inner function output satisfies |y_hat - y| / |y| <= c_g * u_g, where c_g is a nonnegative magnification vector and u_g is the unit round-off (Equation 1 of paper)

verification.spec: Implement a function that, given a mixed forward-backward stable algorithm for g, returns the componentwise bound c_g * u_g; test on small matrix-vector product cases where c_g is known analytically (e.g., c_g = k*|A|*|x|/|Ax| for deterministic rounding); assert the computed bound matches the analytical bound within 5% on at least 3 test cases

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the composition error bound is decomposed via triangle inequality as |z_hat - z| <= |z_hat - f(y_hat)| + |f(y_hat) - f(y)| (Equation in Section 2.1)

verification.spec: Implement a function that computes both terms separately and their sum, then verifies that the sum upper-bounds the true |z_hat - z| for a test composition (e.g., softmax of a matrix-vector product); assert the inequality holds across at least 5 random test inputs

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the Jacobian-based bound |f(y_hat) - f(y)| <= |J_f(y_hat) * diag(y_hat)| * (c_g * u_g) using Taylor's theorem holds for compositional rounding (Equation in Section 2.1)

verification.spec: Implement Jacobian-based bound using f's Jacobian at y_hat scaled by diag(y_hat); test on softmax and RMS LayerNorm where J_f is known analytically; for random y_hat and small perturbations, assert the Jacobian-bound upper-bounds the true |f(y_hat) - f(y)| in 5 test cases

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the combined bound (Equation 2 of paper) |z_hat - z|/|z| <= c_f*u_f + |M(f,y_hat)| * (c_g * u_g / y_hat) holds where M = |J_f(y_hat) * diag(y_hat)|

verification.spec: Implement the combined bound in Eq. (2); test on softmax composition with matrix-vector product input; assert the computed RHS bound upper-bounds the true relative error |z_hat - z|/|z| across 5 random test cases with small u_g, u_f

verification.spec: Run LAMP (Eq. 8) with mu=4 on OpenWebText, once with original token order and once with within-sequence random permutation; sweep tau and plot Pareto (KL vs sparsity, flip rate vs sparsity); at a fixed sparsity (~1%) verify KL divergences agree within 2x and flip rate of permuted is within 2-5x of direct.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: ratio of KL between permuted and direct at fixed sparsity

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the Strict LAMP (Eq. 8) vs Relaxed LAMP (Eq. 9) ablation is reproduced: relaxed LAMP exhibits only a marginal upward shift in its Pareto boundary compared with strict LAMP for GPT-2 XL at mu=4 on OpenWebText (Figure 3).

verification.spec: Implement strict LAMP per Eq. 8 (selecting nonzero q_j where |y_j - sum_i z_i y_i| * z_j (1 - z_j) exceeds threshold using full softmax vector) and relaxed LAMP per Eq. 9 (omit 1 - z_j, use relative threshold); sweep tau and compute Pareto curves over 100 x 1024 OpenWebText tokens at mu=4; at recomputation rate 1% verify relaxed KL divergence is within 2x of strict KL divergence.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: ratio of relaxed-to-strict KL at fixed sparsity

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the length-normalized (LN) variant of relaxed LAMP is reproduced and compared with the original relaxed LAMP (Appendix C.5, Table 1) on at least CodeParrot, showing the LN variant matches perplexity with lower sparsity at tau=0.03 and tau=0.09.

verification.spec: Implement relaxed LAMP (Eq. 9) and its LN variant (scale tau by sqrt(1024/n) per row of causal KQ matrix); run GPT-2 XL with mu=4 on CodeParrot over 100 batches of 1024 tokens at tau in {0.03, 0.09}; verify Relaxed LN sparsity is lower than Relaxed sparsity at the same tau (4.2% < 5.5% at tau=0.03; 1.8% < 2.6% at tau=0.09) and perplexities match within 0.01.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: % sparsity for Relaxed LN at tau=0.03 on CodeParrot

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that LAMP evaluation of KQ inner products in GPT-2 XL attention with mu=7 mantissa bits and threshold tau=0.1 yields a recomputation rate of approximately 0.9% of inner products (within the causal mask) on an OpenWebText sample.

verification.spec: Run GPT-2 XL forward pass on >=10 OpenWebText sequences of length 1024 with the LAMP-attention kernel configured with mu=7 and tau=0.1 (strict LAMP per Eq. 8). Count the number of KQ inner products flagged for FP32 recomputation, divide by the total number of inner products in the causal mask. Assert |measured_rate - 0.009| / 0.009 <= tolerance.
verification.paper_cite: Section 1.2, Figure 1 caption: 'Only 0.9% of them are recomputed in FP32'

sub_tasks.paper_unit: fraction of KQ inner products recomputed

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that adaptive LAMP KQ accumulation with mu=7 (BF16-equivalent) and 0.9% recomputation achieves KL divergence (vs FP32 reference) comparable to uniform TF32 (mu=10) accumulation on OpenWebText with GPT-2 XL.

verification.spec: Compute mean KL divergence between FP32-reference GPT-2 XL and (a) adaptive-LAMP with mu=7, tau=0.1 and (b) uniform mu=10 (TF32) over >=100 OpenWebText sequences of 1024 tokens. Assert that KL_adaptive_mu7 and KL_uniform_mu10 lie within one order of magnitude of each other (ratio between 0.1 and 10).
verification.paper_cite: Section 1.2 and Figure 1: 'BF16 accumulation (mu=7) with adaptive recomputation deviates from uniform FP32 accumulation just as much as uniform TF32 accumulation (mu=10)'

sub_tasks.paper_unit: ratio of KL divergences (adaptive mu=7 vs uniform mu=10)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that randomly choosing the same number of KQ inner products to recompute in FP32 (instead of adaptively per LAMP) yields KL divergence essentially equal to no recomputation at all, confirming that the adaptive choice (not merely the count) drives the accuracy gain.

verification.spec: For mu=7, tau=0.1 on GPT-2 XL with OpenWebText (>=50 sequences x 1024 tokens), compute KL_no_recomp (uniform mu=7) and KL_random_recomp (mu=7 with the same per-row recomputation count as adaptive LAMP, but indices sampled uniformly at random). Assert |log10(KL_random / KL_no_recomp)| <= tolerance, i.e., random recomputation does not improve KL by more than a small factor.
verification.paper_cite: Figure 1 and Appendix C.4 / Figure 7: 'random recomputations do not lead to any improvements'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the custom PS(mu) partial-single floating-point format simulates low-precision matrix-product accumulation by computing each inner product as a sequence of round_to_mu_mantissa_bits(c + a*b) updates with round-to-nearest-ties-to-even, where a, b, c are FP32.

verification.spec: Construct small test vectors a, b (length 32) in FP32. (1) Verify PS(23) accumulation equals FP32 dot product bit-exactly. (2) Verify PS(10) accumulation equals TF32-emulated dot product. (3) Verify PS(7) accumulation equals BF16-accumulator-emulated dot product. (4) Verify the per-step formula: pick mu=5, run the accumulator manually round((round(c+a0*b0, mu) + a1*b1), mu) ... and assert it matches the library implementation bit-exactly.
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.1: 'we accumulate inner products as round(c + a*b), where the scalar multiplication and addition are in FP32'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that, given a low-precision KQ score matrix S_lp (computed in PS(mu)) and a binary selection mask Q of the same shape, the LAMP attention kernel replaces exactly the entries where Q=1 with their FP32-recomputed values and leaves the Q=0 entries untouched.

verification.spec: Generate random Q, K matrices. Compute S_fp32 = Q @ K^T in FP32 and S_lp in PS(mu=4). Construct an arbitrary boolean mask M (e.g., a fixed pattern). Call the LAMP merge function with (S_lp, Q, K, M). Assert: output[M] == S_fp32[M] bit-exactly, and output[~M] == S_lp[~M] bit-exactly. Also test the case M=all_false (output equals S_lp) and M=all_true (output equals S_fp32).
verification.paper_cite: Algorithm 1, step 'Recompute components of y_hat indexed by nonzeros of q more accurately'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the LAMP attention kernel accepts standard batched causal-attention tensors of shape (B, H, N, N) for the KQ score matrix (with B=batch, H=heads, N=sequence length) and produces outputs consistent with HuggingFace GPT-2 XL attention on OpenWebText inputs.

verification.spec: Load GPT-2 XL via HuggingFace transformers. Replace its attention with the LAMP-attention module configured with mu=23 (full FP32) and any tau. Run forward on a batch of shape (B=2, seq_len=1024) of OpenWebText tokens. Assert: (1) attention scores tensor has shape (2, 25, 1024, 1024) matching GPT-2 XL's 25 heads; (2) output logits match the stock GPT-2 XL FP32 logits to within 1e-3 max abs error; (3) the kernel correctly applies the causal mask (upper-triangular entries do not affect output).
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.2: 'GPT-2 XL model... 100 sequences of 1024 tokens each... causal mask'
